question: What is Know Your Customer (KYC)?
answer: The Reserve Bank of India (Know Your Customer (KYC)) Directions, 2016, [1] outlines the guidelines for Know Your Customer (KYC) policy, which is a crucial aspect of preventing money laundering and terrorist financing [2]. The KYC policy includes four key elements: Customer Acceptance Policy, Risk Management, Customer Identification Procedures (CIP), and Monitoring of Transactions [3]. The KYC process involves verifying the identity of customers through a digital KYC process [4], which includes capturing live photographs of the customer and their original documents, and validating the information through a One Time Password (OTP) [4]. The overall objective of KYC is to ensure that banks and other regulated entities (REs) have a thorough understanding of their customers and can effectively manage the risks associated with money laundering and terrorist financing [2].
reference_answer: KYC is a process by which a Regulated Entity (RE), including a bank, obtains information on identity and address of the customer, nature of business and financial status of a customer and, verifies the same. This process helps to ensure that an RE is aware of the customer it is dealing with, and the services provided by the RE are not misused for Money Laundering/ Terrorist Financing/ Proliferation Financing (ML/TF/PF) purposes.

question: Is KYC mandatory? When a RE is required to carry out KYC?
answer: KYC is mandatory for all customers [3]. A RE is required to carry out KYC at the time of commencement of an account-based relationship [3] and during periodic updation, which shall be carried out at least once in every two years for high-risk customers, once in every eight years for medium risk customers and once in every ten years for low-risk customers from the date of opening of the account / last KYC updation [1]. Additionally, KYC shall be carried out when there is a change in the customer's information [1] or when the customer's account is due for periodic updation [1]. The RE shall also apply the CDD procedure at the UCIC level [3], and no transaction or account-based relationship shall be undertaken without following the CDD procedure [3].
reference_answer: Yes, KYC is mandatorily required to be carried out: (a) at the time of commencement of an account-based relationship, i.e., opening any type of account with the RE; or (b) when a walk-in-customer carries out occasional transaction of an amount equal to or exceeding ₹50,000 (whether conducted as a single transaction or several transactions that appear to be connected); or (c) when a walk-in-customer carries out any international money transfer operations; or (d) when the RE has a doubt about the authenticity or adequacy of the customer identification data it has obtained; or (e) when the RE sells its own products or third party products as an agent; payment of dues of credit cards/ sale and reloading of prepaid/ travel cards and any other product for more than ₹50,000.

question: Who is a walk-in-customer?
answer: A walk-in customer is a person who does not have an account-based relationship with the Regulated Entity (RE), but undertakes transactions with the RE [5]. This is further clarified in paragraph 13 of Chapter V, where it is stated that REs shall undertake identification of customers, including walk-in customers, when the amount involved is equal to or exceeds rupees fifty thousand, whether conducted as a single transaction or several transactions that appear to be connected [2].
reference_answer: A “Walk-in Customer” means a person who does not have an account-based relationship with the RE, but undertakes transactions with the RE.

question: Does a customer have to furnish KYC documents for each account he opens with the same RE?
answer: No, a customer does not have to furnish KYC documents for each account he opens with the same RE [1]. According to Para 10 of the Master Direction KYC 2016, REs shall apply the CDD procedure at the UCIC level, which means that if an existing KYC compliant customer of a RE desires to open another account or avail any other product or service from the same RE, there shall be no need for a fresh CDD exercise as far as identification of the customer is concerned [1].
reference_answer: No, if an existing KYC compliant customer of an RE desires to open another account or avail any other product/ service from the same RE, he is not required to submit any KYC document unless there is a change in the information with respect to his identity and/ or address.

question: What are the documents required for opening a bank account by an individual?
answer: For opening a 'Small Account' by an individual, the bank shall obtain a self-attested photograph from the customer [1]. The designated officer of the bank certifies under his signature that the person opening the account has affixed his signature or thumb impression in his presence [1]. However, the provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully for a regular individual account, as the requirements for a 'Small Account' are specified, but not the general requirements for an individual account.
reference_answer: Following documents are required for opening of an account by an individual: (a) any one of the following Officially Valid Documents (OVDs) or the equivalent e- document thereof, containing details of his name and address, viz., • the passport, • the driving licence, • proof of possession of Aadhaar number, • the Voter's Identity Card issued by the Election Commission of India, • the job card issued by NREGA duly signed by an officer of the State Government, and • the letter issued by the National Population Register. (b) the Permanent Account Number (PAN) or the equivalent e-document, thereof, or Form No. 60 as defined in Income-tax Rules, 1962; and (c) other documents in respect of the nature of business and financial status of the customer, or the equivalent e-documents thereof, as may be required by the RE.

question: What is a ‘Small Account’?
answer: A ‘Small Account’ is a type of bank account that can be opened by an individual with certain limitations, as an alternative to the requirements specified in paragraph 16 [1]. The limitations include: the aggregate of all credits in a financial year does not exceed rupees one lakh; the aggregate of all withdrawals and transfers in a month does not exceed rupees ten thousand; and the balance at any point of time does not exceed rupees fifty thousand [1]. This type of account is subject to specific conditions, including obtaining a self-attested photograph from the customer and certification by a designated officer of the bank [1].
reference_answer: In case a customer does not possess an OVD, he can still open a ‘Small Account’ with a bank. For the purpose, the bank shall obtain a self-attested photograph from the customer and the designated officer of the bank certifies under his signature that the person opening the account has affixed his signature or thumb impression in his presence. The Small Account shall remain operational initially for a period of twelve months and is subject to conditions as given under paragraph 23 of the Master Direction on KYC.

question: What are deemed OVDs for the purpose of proof of address?
answer: For the purpose of proof of address, deemed OVDs are defined in paragraph (a)(xiv) [2].
reference_answer: The customer can furnish one of the following documents or the equivalent e- documents thereof, if the OVD submitted by him to the RE does not contain current address. These documents are deemed to be OVDs for the limited purpose of proof of address: (a) utility bill which is not more than two months old of any service provider (electricity, telephone, post-paid mobile phone, piped gas, water bill); (b) property or Municipal tax receipt; (c) pension or family pension payment orders (PPOs) issued to retired employees by Government Departments or Public Sector Undertakings, if they contain the address; 2 (d) letter of allotment of accommodation from employer issued by State Government or Central Government Departments, statutory or regulatory bodies, public sector undertakings, scheduled commercial banks, financial institutions and listed companies and leave and licence agreements with such employers allotting official accommodation. However, the customer shall submit OVD with current address within a period of three months of submitting the document specified above.

question: If a customer is staying, say, in Chennai but his OVD contains the address of, say, New Delhi, can he open an account in Chennai?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully. However, according to [4](d), the mandatory information to be sought for KYC purpose while opening an account includes proof of address [4]. Additionally, [5](a) mentions that a declaration about the local address shall be obtained within a period of 30 days of opening the account and the said local address is verified [5]. But it does not explicitly state what happens if the address on the OVD is different from the current address.
reference_answer: Yes. The customer can open an account with a RE by submitting a deemed to be OVD for the purpose of proof of address. However, as mentioned in reply to Q 7 above, he is required to submit OVD with current address within a period of three months.

question: If there is a change in the name and the OVD does not include the new name, how can one open an account?
answer: If there is a change in the name and the OVD does not include the new name, the provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully [1][2][3][4][5].
reference_answer: In such case, an OVD containing the earlier name along with a copy of the Gazette notification, or marriage certificate issued by the State Government, as applicable, indicating the change in name, can be submitted for opening the account.

question: Is Aadhaar number mandatory for purposes of KYC?
answer: The Aadhaar number is not mandatory for purposes of KYC [2]. According to Para 16, Chapter VI Part I, Master Direction KYC 2016, the customer may submit his Aadhaar number voluntarily to a bank or any RE notified under the first proviso to sub-section (1) of section A of the PML Act [2]. Alternatively, the customer may provide proof of possession of Aadhaar number or any other officially valid document (OVD) or the equivalent e-document thereof containing the details of his identity and address [2].
reference_answer: No. However, in case the customer is desirous of receiving any benefit or subsidy under any scheme notified under section 7 of the Aadhaar (Targeted Delivery of Financial and Other subsidies, Benefits and Services) Act, 2016 (18 of 2016), the customer shall provide the Aadhaar number. In other cases, the Aadhaar number may be provided voluntarily by the customers.

question: Is fresh KYC needed where a customer transfers his existing account with an RE from one branch/ office of the RE to another?
answer: No, fresh KYC is not needed where a customer transfers his existing account with an RE from one branch/ office of the RE to another, provided full KYC verification has already been done for the concerned account and the same is not due for periodic updation [1].
reference_answer: KYC verification once done by one branch/ office of the RE shall be valid for transfer of the account to any other branch/ office of the same RE, provided full KYC verification has already been done for the concerned account and the same is not due for periodic updation.

question: Whether KYC is required to be done for all account holders of a joint account?
answer: KYC procedure is required to be followed for all the joint account holders, while opening a joint account [2]. This implies that each account holder in a joint account must undergo the KYC process, as part of the Customer Due Diligence (CDD) procedure [2].
reference_answer: Yes, documents as mentioned in reply to Q 5 above shall be submitted by all account holders of a joint account to the RE. 3

question: What are the different modes for onboarding individual customers by the RE?
answer: The different modes for onboarding individual customers by the RE include face-to-face mode, non-face-to-face mode using digital channels such as CKYCR, DigiLocker, equivalent e-document, etc. [1], and non-digital modes such as obtaining a copy of OVD certified by additional certifying authorities as allowed for NRIs and PIOs [1]. Additionally, REs may also undertake Video-based Customer Identification Process (V-CIP) for onboarding individual customers [2].
reference_answer: An RE has the following options to onboard a customer: (a) Face-to-face onboarding: • Visit to the branch/ office of the RE; • using e-KYC authentication (OTP as well as biometric based authentication); undertaking offline verification of proof of possession of Aadhaar Number; obtaining certified copy of the OVD or equivalent e-document thereof; undertaking ‘Digital KYC Process’, as per paragraph 16 of the MD on KYC. • Video based Customer Identification Process (V-CIP) complying with prescribed standards and procedures. (b) Non-face-to-face onboarding: • using Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC authentication; • using digital channels such as CKYCR, DigiLocker, equivalent e-document, etc., and non-digital modes such as obtaining copy of OVD certified by additional certifying authorities as allowed for NRIs and PIOs.

question: What is a KYC Identifier? How is it useful for a customer?
answer: A KYC Identifier is generated by the Central KYC Records Registry (CKYCR) [5] once the KYC records of a customer are uploaded by a Reporting Entity (RE). The KYC Identifier is useful for a customer as it enables them to provide their KYC details to other REs without having to submit the same KYC records or information again [5]. This is because the RE can retrieve the customer's KYC records online using the KYC Identifier, unless there is a change in the customer's information, the retrieved records are incomplete, or the RE considers it necessary for verification or enhanced due diligence [5]. The customer can provide their KYC Identifier to the RE, which can then use it to obtain the customer's KYC records from the CKYCR [5].
reference_answer: KYC Identifier means the unique number or code assigned to a customer of an RE by the Central KYC Records Registry (CKYCR). If the customer approaches an RE for opening an account, he can provide his KYC Identifier and give consent to the RE to download the valid KYC data from CKYCR. This can obviate the need for the customer to submit KYC documents again while opening an account with another RE.

question: Can the KYC Identifier be used for any other purpose?
answer: The KYC Identifier can be used for the purpose of establishing an account-based relationship, updation/ periodic updation or for verification of identity of a customer [5]. The RE shall seek the KYC Identifier from the customer or retrieve the KYC Identifier, if available, from the CKYCR and proceed to obtain KYC records online by using such KYC Identifier [5]. However, the Master Direction does not explicitly state that the KYC Identifier can be used for any other purpose beyond these specified uses [5].
reference_answer: In addition to the purpose of establishing an account-based relationship, KYC Identifier can be used for updation/ periodic updation of KYC, or for verification of identity of a customer. Further details in this regard are given in paragraph 56(j) of the Master Direction on KYC.

question: Whether customers’ consent is required for opening of account using KYC identifier?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully [1][2][3][4][5]. However, it can be noted that specific consent from the customer is required for authentication through OTP for accounts opened using Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode [1]. Additionally, the CDD procedure, which includes obtaining customer information and consent, is a crucial aspect of the KYC process [2]. Furthermore, the use of KYC identifier for verification of identity of a customer is mentioned, but the requirement of customer consent for this specific purpose is not explicitly stated [5].
reference_answer: Yes, the RE is required to obtain customer’s explicit consent to use his/ her KYC Identifier for downloading KYC records from CKYCR for the purposes mentioned in reply to Q. 14 and 15.

question: How can a customer obtain his KYC Identifier issued by CKYCR?
answer: A customer can obtain his KYC Identifier issued by CKYCR when the RE ensures that the same is communicated to the individual once it is generated by CKYCR [1]. Additionally, the customer can also retrieve the KYC Identifier from the CKYCR, and the RE can retrieve the KYC records online from the CKYCR in accordance with paragraph 56, using the KYC Identifier with the explicit consent of the customer [1][2].
reference_answer: A customer can obtain his KYC Identifier through the following ways: 4 (a) In the process of opening an account, once the customer’s KYC Identifier is generated by CKYCR and provided to the RE, the latter shall share the same with the concerned customer. (b) The customer can also access his KYC Identifier on CKYCR Portal (www.ckycindia.in).

question: It is easy to use KYC Identifier for opening an account. Whether any measure has been taken to improve its use by the REs?
answer: The use of KYC Identifier has been facilitated by the Central KYC Records Registry (CKYCR) [4]. REs can retrieve the updated KYC records from CKYCR once they are informed about an update in the KYC record of an existing customer [4]. Additionally, REs can seek the KYC Identifier from the customer or retrieve it from the CKYCR and proceed to obtain KYC records online by using such KYC Identifier [4]. This simplifies the process of opening an account and reduces the need for customers to submit the same KYC records or information multiple times [4]. Furthermore, REs are required to upload KYC records pertaining to accounts of individual customers and Legal Entities (LEs) to CKYCR, which helps to ensure that all KYC records are incrementally uploaded [4]. The ultimate responsibility for knowing the customer lies with the RE [5].
reference_answer: For the purpose of opening an account, RE is required to seek the KYC Identifier from the customer or retrieve the same, if available, from the CKYCR and obtain KYC records by using such KYC Identifier. In such cases, the customer is not required to submit the same KYC records/ information/ any other additional identification documents, unless– (a) there is a change in the information of the customer as existing in the records of CKYCR; or (b) the KYC record or information retrieved is incomplete or is not as per the current applicable KYC norms; or (c) the validity period of downloaded documents has lapsed; or (d) the RE considers it necessary in order to verify the identity or address (including current address) of the customer, or to perform enhanced due diligence or to build an appropriate risk profile of the customer.

question: What is Video-based Customer Identification Process (V-CIP)?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully. [1][2][3]
reference_answer: V-CIP is an alternate method of customer identification with facial recognition and customer due diligence that allows REs to obtain and verify a customer’s identity information through a digital, secure, live, informed and consent-based and live audio- visual interaction between an authorised RE official and the customer to obtain identification information required for CDD purpose. V-CIP eliminates the need for physical visit to a branch of the RE but is treated on par with face-to-face CIP. Assisted V-CIP is also permitted when banks take help of Business Correspondents (BCs) facilitating the process only at the customer end.

question: Whether blinking of eyes or making specific facial gestures are mandatory to complete V-CIP?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully [1][2][3].
reference_answer: : No, making specific facial gesture like blinking of eyes, smiling, frowning, etc. are not mandatory for liveness check. The RE is required to take due cognizance of special need, if any, of the customer during liveness check. 5

question: What are the purposes for which V-CIP can be used by an RE?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully, as it does not mention V-CIP (Video-based Customer Identification Process) or its purposes [1][2][3].

question: Why does an RE periodically ask for KYC documents from the customer when the customer has already submitted the same during opening of account?
answer: An RE periodically asks for KYC documents from the customer when the customer has already submitted the same during opening of account to ensure that the information or data collected under CDD is kept up-to-date and relevant [2]. This is done to adopt a risk-based approach for periodic updation of KYC, particularly where there is high risk [2]. The REs shall ensure that the KYC documents of the customer as per the current CDD standards are available with them, and this is applicable even if there is no change in customer information but the documents available with the RE are not as per the current CDD standards [2]. Additionally, the REs shall verify the customer's PAN details, if available, from the database of the issuing authority at the time of periodic updation of KYC [2].
reference_answer: RE is required to update the customers’ KYC records as part of ongoing due diligence process to ensure that the information or data collected under CDD is kept up to date and relevant. The periodicity of such updation depends on the risk categorisation of the customer by the RE and such periodic updation of KYC records (at times referred to as re-KYC) shall be carried out at least once in every two years for high-risk, eight years for medium risk and ten years for low-risk customers. However, RE may adopt any additional and exceptional measures as per its internal KYC policy which inter alia may require physical presence of the customer, periodic updation of KYC only in the branch of the RE where account is maintained, a more frequent periodicity of KYC updation than the minimum specified periodicity, etc.

question: What are the parameters used by the RE for risk categorisation of a customer?
answer: The parameters used by the RE for risk categorisation of a customer include customer’s identity, social/financial status, nature of business activity, and information about the customer’s business and their location, geographical risk covering customers as well as transactions, type of products/services offered, delivery channel used for delivery of products/services, types of transaction undertaken – cash, cheque/monetary instruments, wire transfers, forex transactions, etc. [1]. Additionally, the ability to confirm identity documents through online or other services offered by issuing authorities may also be factored in [1].
reference_answer: The broad indicative parameters prescribed for customer’s risk categorisation are customer’s identity, social/ financial status, nature of business activity, information about the customer’s business and his/ her location, geographical risk covering customer as well as transactions, type of products/ services offered, delivery channel used for delivery of products/ services, types of transaction undertaken – cash, cheque/monetary instruments, wire transfers, forex transactions, etc. While considering customer’s identity, the ability to confirm identity documents through online or other services offered by issuing authorities may also be factored in. The risk category and reasons thereof are not required to be divulged to the customers by the RE.

question: *What are the modes for periodic updation of KYC for individual customers?
answer: For individual customers, the modes for periodic updation of KYC include obtaining a self-declaration from the customer through various channels such as email-id, mobile number, ATMs, digital channels, or letter [1]. Additionally, in case of a change only in the address details, a self-declaration of the new address shall be obtained and verified through positive confirmation within two months [1]. Furthermore, Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face to face mode may also be used for periodic updation of KYC [1]. Video-based Customer Identification Process (V-CIP) can also be undertaken for periodic updation of KYC for eligible customers [2].
reference_answer: The modes for periodic updation of KYC for individuals are as under: 6 (a) It can be undertaken at the branch of the RE with which customer maintains the account or any other branch of the same RE; or (b) RE can obtain a self-declaration when there is “no change in KYC information” and also when there is “change only in address details”, through customer’s email-id/ mobile number registered with the RE, ATMs, digital channels (such as online banking / internet banking, mobile application of RE), letter, etc. However, the declared address shall be verified by the RE through positive confirmation within two months; or (c) Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face-to-face (NF2F) mode and V-CIP; (d) RE is also mandated to update customer’s KYC records based on the update information/ notification, if any, received from CKYCR for the customer concerned.

question: What documents are needed for periodic updation of KYC?
answer: For periodic updation of KYC, the documents needed include KYC documents as per the current CDD standards [1]. In case of no change in KYC information, a self-declaration from the customer is required [1]. If there is a change in address, a self-declaration of the new address is needed, and the declared address shall be verified through positive confirmation within two months [1]. Additionally, REs may obtain a copy of OVD or deemed OVD, or the equivalent e-documents thereof, for the purpose of proof of address [1]. For customers other than individuals, a self-declaration and update of Beneficial Ownership (BO) information are required [1]. The REs shall also ensure that the customer's PAN details are verified from the database of the issuing authority [1]. Furthermore, the REs shall obtain an update of documents submitted by the customer within 30 days of the update [1].
reference_answer: The requirement of KYC documents depends on the modes used for periodic updation of KYC as detailed in the reply to Q 24, above. (a) In case of change in the KYC information, the customer has to provide the documents as mentioned in the reply to Q 5 above; (b) In case of no change in KYC information, the customer can provide a self- declaration. Similarly, a self-declaration in case of change only in address can also be provided by the customer to RE as detailed in reply to Q 24 above. (c) In case of Aadhaar OTP-based e-KYC authentication facility in non-face-to-face mode, the customer can provide a declaration of the current address even if the current address is different from the address in Aadhaar database. However, RE shall ensure that mobile number for Aadhaar authentication is same as the one available with it in the customer’s profile, in order to prevent any fraud. (d) In case of use of V-CIP for completing re-KYC, the requirements shall be as per paragraph 18 of the Master Direction on KYC, which include identification and verification of the identity using (i) Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC authentication in non-face-to-face mode; (ii) offline verification of Aadhaar; (iii) KYC records downloaded from CKYCR; and (iv) equivalent e-documents including documents issued through DigiLocker. 7

question: Can the customer get an acknowledgment from the RE after submitting the KYC documents for periodic updation of KYC?
answer: Yes, the customer can get an acknowledgment from the RE after submitting the KYC documents for periodic updation of KYC [1]. The RE shall provide an acknowledgment to the customer mentioning the date of receipt of the relevant document(s), including self-declaration from the customer, for carrying out updation/ periodic updation [1].
reference_answer: Yes, once a customer submits updated KYC information or self-declaration to the RE, it shall provide an acknowledgement to this effect to the customer. Upon updating KYC information, the RE shall also intimate customers about such updation.

question: Are the REs required to give due notices to the customer for completing his pending periodic updation of KYC?
answer: Yes, the REs are required to give due notices to the customer for completing his pending periodic updation of KYC [1]. The RE shall intimate its customers, in advance, to update their KYC, and prior to the due date of periodic updation of KYC, the RE shall give at least three advance intimations, including at least one intimation by letter, at appropriate intervals to its customers through available communication options/channels for complying with the requirement of periodic updation of KYC [1]. Subsequent to the due date, the RE shall give at least three reminders, including at least one reminder by letter, at appropriate intervals, to such customers who have still not complied with the requirements, despite advance intimations [1].
reference_answer: Yes, the RE shall intimate its customer, in advance, to comply with the requirement of periodic updation of KYC. Subsequent to the due date, RE shall also give reminders to such customer who has still not complied with the requirements, despite advance intimations.

question: What happens if periodic updation of KYC is not done even after advance intimations/ reminders?
answer: If periodic updation of KYC is not done even after advance intimations/ reminders, the consequences, if any, of failure to update their KYC in time shall be clearly mentioned in the letter of intimation/ reminder [1]. However, the provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully regarding specific consequences.
reference_answer: Prevention of Money-Laundering (Maintenance of Records) Rules, 2005 enable the REs to obtain the identity records (including updated records of identity) from the customers, failing which the RE shall close the account of the customers after giving due notice.

question: What should a customer do if he receives SMS/ email with links to complete periodic updation of KYC?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully [1]. However, according to the Master Direction KYC 2016, a self-declaration from the customer can be obtained through customer’s email-id registered with the RE, customer’s mobile number registered with the RE, ATMs, digital channels [1]. But, it does not explicitly state what a customer should do if they receive an SMS or email with links to complete periodic updation of KYC.
reference_answer: The customer shall exercise utmost caution before clicking links embedded in such SMS/ email as these may be suspicious/ fraudulent. The public is advised not to fall prey to frauds being perpetrated in the name of KYC updation. Please refer to the Press Release dated September 13, 2021 on RBI cautions against frauds in the name of KYC updation.

question: Can a person purchase a Demand Draft/ Payment Order/ Travellers Cheque against cash?
answer: No, a person cannot purchase a Demand Draft, Payment Order, or Travellers Cheque against cash for a value of rupees fifty thousand and above [1]. Such remittances shall be effected by debit to the customer's account or against cheques and not against cash payment [1].
reference_answer: Demand Draft/ Payment Order/ Travellers Cheques for amount less than ₹50,000 can be purchased against cash. However, such instrument for ₹50,000 and above can be issued only by way of debiting the customer’s account or against cheques.

question: What is the period of presenting payment instruments including cheques/ drafts/ pay orders/ banker’s cheques?
answer: The period for presenting payment instruments, including cheques, drafts, pay orders, and banker's cheques, is three months from the date of such instruments [1]. Payment of these instruments shall not be made if they are presented beyond this period [1].
reference_answer: Payment of cheque/ draft/ pay order/ banker’s cheque, if presented beyond the period of three months from the date of issuance of such instrument, shall not be made by RE. 8

question: What should a customer do in case the documents submitted by the customer, at the time of establishing an account-based relationship (opening an account with an RE), have been modified/ updated/ renewed?
answer: In case the documents submitted by the customer at the time of establishing an account-based relationship have been modified/updated/renewed, the customer should submit the updated documents to the RE [1]. However, the provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully, as it does not explicitly state the procedure for updating documents. Nevertheless, it can be inferred that the customer should provide the updated documents to ensure that the RE has the most recent and accurate information about the customer [1][5]. Additionally, the RE shall undertake identification of customers when there is a doubt about the authenticity or adequacy of the customer identification data it has obtained [1].
reference_answer: In case of any change in the OVDs such as change in demographic information or address or other documents submitted by a customer at the time of establishing account- based relationship, the customer shall submit the updated OVD/ other documents to the RE within 30 days of the update/ change/ modification in the documents for the purpose of updating the records at RE’s end. In such case, the RE shall acknowledge the receipt of the KYC documents to the customer.

question: Whether all modes of customer onboarding (i.e., opening of account) as mentioned in the reply to Q 13 are available to the person with disabilities (PwD)?
answer: All modes of customer onboarding are available to persons with disabilities (PwDs) [4]. The Customer Acceptance Policy shall not result in denial of banking/financial facility to members of the general public, especially those who are financially or socially disadvantaged, including the Persons with Disabilities (PwDs) [4]. However, specific provisions for PwDs are not detailed in the provided context, but it is reiterated that no application for onboarding or periodic updation of KYC shall be rejected without application of mind [4].
reference_answer: : Yes, all modes of account opening as mentioned in the reply to Q 13 above, viz. onboarding customer in face-to-face mode; non-face-to-face mode and V-CIP are available to the person with disabilities.

question: Whether the application of KYC/ periodic updation of KYC of a customer can be rejected automatically?
answer: No, the application of KYC/ periodic updation of KYC of a customer cannot be rejected automatically [1]. According to Para 11, Chapter III, Master Direction KYC 2016, no application for onboarding or periodic updation of KYC shall be rejected without application of mind, and the reason(s) of rejection shall be duly recorded by the officer concerned [1].
reference_answer: REs’ Customer Acceptance Policy shall not result in denial of banking/ financial facility to members of the general public, especially those, who are financially or socially disadvantaged, including the persons with disabilities (PWDs). The decision to reject the application of KYC/ periodic updation of KYC shall not be automated and such decisions on rejection shall be reviewed by an official of RE authorised for the purpose.

question: Whether thumb impression in place of signature would be accepted for KYC purposes?
answer: Yes, thumb impression in place of signature would be accepted for KYC purposes [1][4]. According to paragraph 24 and paragraph 23, the designated officer of the NBFC or bank certifies under his signature that the person opening the account has affixed his signature or thumb impression in his presence [1][4]. This indicates that thumb impression is an acceptable alternative to signature for KYC purposes.
reference_answer: : The provision to accept thumb impression in place of signatures has already been prescribed for onboarding customer in face-to-face-mode under paragraph 16 and 23 of the MD on KYC.

question: What is the beneficial ownership threshold for a company under the KYC Master Direction?
answer:
The beneficial ownership threshold for a company is not explicitly stated in the provided context [1]. However, for a partnership firm, the beneficial owner is the natural person(s) who has/have ownership of/entitlement to more than 10 percent of capital or profits of the partnership [1]. For an unincorporated association or body of individuals, the beneficial owner is the natural person(s) who has/have ownership of/entitlement to more than 15 percent of the property or capital or profits [1]. For a trust, the identification of beneficial owner(s) includes the author of the trust, the trustee, the beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust [1]. 

The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully for a company.
reference_answer: Controlling ownership interest means ownership of or entitlement to more than 10 percent of the shares or capital or profits of the company.

question: What is the beneficial ownership threshold for a partnership firm?
answer: The beneficial owner of a partnership firm is the natural person(s) who has/have ownership of/entitlement to more than 10 percent of capital or profits of the partnership [1].
reference_answer: More than 10 percent of capital or profits of the partnership.

question: What is the beneficial ownership threshold for an unincorporated association or body of individuals?
answer: For an unincorporated association or body of individuals, the beneficial owner is the natural person(s) who has/have ownership of/entitlement to more than 15 percent of the property or capital or profits of the unincorporated association or body of individuals [2].
reference_answer: More than 15 percent of the property or capital or profits.

question: Who qualifies as beneficial owner when the customer is a trust?
answer: When the customer is a trust, the beneficial owner(s) include the author of the trust, the trustee, the beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership [1]. Additionally, for opening an account of a trust, certified copies of documents such as the trust deed, permanent account number or Form No.60 of the trust, and documents relating to beneficial owners, managers, officers or employees, as well as the names of the beneficiaries, trustees, settlor, protector, if any, and authors of the trust, shall be obtained [5]. Furthermore, the identification of beneficial owners of a trust is also subject to the guidelines outlined in paragraph 34, which provides that for opening an account of a legal person, including a trust, the beneficial owner(s) shall be identified and all reasonable steps shall be undertaken to verify his/her identity [2].
reference_answer: The author of the trust, the trustee, beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest, and any person exercising ultimate effective control.

question: What does ongoing due diligence mean under the KYC directions?
answer: Ongoing due diligence under the KYC directions means that REs shall undertake continuous monitoring of customers to ensure that their transactions are consistent with their knowledge about the customers, customers’ business and risk profile, the source of funds / wealth [5]. This may involve monitoring certain types of transactions, such as large and complex transactions, transactions with unusual patterns, and high account turnover inconsistent with the size of the balance maintained [4]. REs may also consider adopting innovations like artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI & ML) technologies to support effective monitoring [4]. The ultimate goal of ongoing due diligence is to prevent money laundering, terrorist financing, and other related risks, and to ensure compliance with the PML Act and regulatory instructions [3].
reference_answer: Regular monitoring of transactions to ensure those are consistent with the RE's knowledge about the customer's business, risk profile, and source of funds or wealth.

question: What is a suspicious transaction as defined in the Master Direction?
answer: The provided context does not contain a definition of a suspicious transaction [1][2][3][4][5]. However, it is mentioned that REs shall have effective risk-based policies and procedures for determining when to execute, reject, or suspend a wire transfer lacking required originator or required beneficiary information, and if the transaction is found to be suspicious, reporting to FIU-IND in accordance with the PML Rules [1]. Additionally, it is stated that every RE, its directors, officers, and all employees shall ensure that the fact of maintenance of records and furnishing of the information to the Director is confidential, but such confidentiality requirement shall not inhibit sharing of information under paragraph (b) of this Master Direction of any analysis of transactions and activities which appear unusual, if any such analysis has been done [2].

question: How are branches of Indian banks abroad required to comply with KYC standards?
answer: Branches of Indian banks abroad are required to comply with KYC standards [1]. They must adopt the more stringent regulation of the two, i.e., the standards prescribed by the Reserve Bank of India and the host country regulators [1]. However, if the applicable laws and regulations in the host country prohibit the implementation of these guidelines, the bank must bring this to the notice of the Reserve Bank of India, which may advise further necessary action, including the application of additional measures to manage ML/TF risks [1].
reference_answer: They must adopt the more stringent of RBI and host country standards. If host country laws prohibit RBI guidelines, the matter must be brought to RBI's notice.

question: What is a payable-through account under the KYC directions?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully.
reference_answer: Correspondent accounts used directly by third parties to transact business on their own behalf.

question: What is Digital KYC as defined in the Master Direction?
answer: Digital KYC is specified under Annex I [5]. However, the provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully, as Annex I is not included in the given context.
reference_answer: Capturing a live photo of the customer and the OVD along with the latitude and longitude of the location where the photo is taken by an authorised officer of the RE.

question: What is an equivalent e-document under the KYC directions?
answer: An equivalent e-document under the KYC directions refers to a digital version of a document that is accepted as a valid proof of identity or address [1]. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) allows the use of equivalent e-documents, such as a digitally signed document or a document with a digital signature, as a substitute for physical documents [2]. For instance, a Permanent Account Number (PAN) card or a proof of address document can be submitted in electronic form, and the digital signature on such documents shall be verified by the RE as per the provisions of the Information Technology Act, 2000 [2]. Additionally, the RBI has specified that an equivalent e-document can be used for proof of business or activity in the name of a proprietary firm [3]. The use of equivalent e-documents is subject to certain conditions and requirements, such as verification of the digital signature and authentication of the document [1][2].
reference_answer: An electronic equivalent of a document issued by the issuing authority with its valid digital signature, including documents issued via DigiLocker.

question: What is a cross-border wire transfer under the Master Direction?
answer: A cross-border wire transfer refers to a transfer of funds from one country to another [1]. According to the Master Direction, all cross-border wire transfers shall be accompanied by accurate, complete, and meaningful originator and beneficiary information, including the name of the originator, the originator account number, the originator’s address or national identity number, the name of the beneficiary, and the beneficiary account number [1]. This information is required to ensure that the transfer is properly traced and monitored, and to prevent money laundering and terrorist financing [1].
reference_answer: Any wire transfer where the ordering and beneficiary financial institutions are in different countries, including any chain where at least one institution is abroad.

question: What is the definition of Customer Due Diligence (CDD) under the KYC directions?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully [1]. However, Chapter VI of the Master Direction KYC 2016 is dedicated to Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure [1]. Additionally, various paragraphs such as 16 [5], 17 [4], 38 [3], and 40 [2] provide details on CDD for different scenarios, including face-to-face and non-face-to-face customer onboarding, and periodic updation of KYC. But a specific definition of Customer Due Diligence is not explicitly mentioned in the given context.
reference_answer: Identifying and verifying the customer and the beneficial owner using reliable and independent sources, including understanding the nature of business and determining if the customer acts on behalf of a beneficial owner.

question: What is CKYCR and what is its function?
answer: The Central KYC Records Registry (CKYCR) is an entity defined under Rule (1) of the Rules [5], which has been authorised to the Central Registry of Securitisation Asset Reconstruction and Security Interest of India (CERSAI) [1]. The function of CKYCR is to receive, store, safeguard and retrieve the KYC records in digital form of a customer [5]. CKYCR generates a unique KYC Identifier for each customer [1] and updates the KYC records of existing customers when new or updated information is received from Reporting Entities (REs) [1].
reference_answer: Central KYC Records Registry — an entity to receive, store, safeguard and retrieve the KYC records in digital form of a customer.

question: What is Periodic Updation as defined in the KYC directions?
answer: Periodic Updation of KYC refers to the process of updating customer information at regular intervals to ensure that the information or data collected under Customer Due Diligence (CDD) is kept up-to-date and relevant [1]. This process is to be carried out at least once in every two years for high-risk customers, once in every eight years for medium risk customers, and once in every ten years for low-risk customers from the date of opening of the account / last KYC updation [1]. The ultimate goal of periodic updation is to prevent money laundering and terrorist financing by ensuring that the customer's information is accurate and current [2].
reference_answer: Steps taken to ensure documents, data or information collected under the CDD process is kept up-to-date and relevant by undertaking reviews at the periodicity prescribed by the Reserve Bank.

question: What is the re-KYC frequency difference between high-risk and low-risk customers, and what documents does each require?
answer: The re-KYC frequency difference between high-risk and low-risk customers is that high-risk customers require periodic updation at least once in every two years, while low-risk customers require periodic updation at least once in every ten years [1]. For high-risk customers, the documents required are not explicitly stated, but it is mentioned that the information or data collected under CDD should be kept up-to-date and relevant [1]. For low-risk customers, in case of no change in KYC information, a self-declaration from the customer is required [1], and in case of a change only in the address details, a self-declaration of the new address and verification of the declared address through positive confirmation within two months is required [1]. Additionally, REs may obtain a copy of OVD or deemed OVD for the purpose of proof of address, as specified in their internal KYC policy [1].
reference_answer: High-risk: at least every 2 years. Low-risk: every 10 years. If info changed, OVDs required; if no change, self-declaration suffices.

question: Can a person with disability use V-CIP to open an account, and are specific facial gestures required during liveness check?
answer: The provided context does not contain enough information to answer this question fully. [1][2][3]
reference_answer: Yes, V-CIP is available to PwDs. Specific facial gestures like blinking or smiling are NOT mandatory; the RE must consider the customer's special needs.

question: When a customer uses their KYC Identifier to open an account, in what circumstances must they still submit fresh KYC documents?
answer: When a customer uses their KYC Identifier to open an account, they must still submit fresh KYC documents in the following circumstances: (i) there is a change in the information of the customer as existing in the records of CKYCR [5]; (ii) the KYC record or information retrieved is incomplete or is not as per the current applicable KYC norms [5]; (iii) the validity period of downloaded documents has lapsed [5]; or (iv) the RE considers it necessary in order to verify the identity or address (including current address) of the customer, or to perform enhanced due diligence or to build an appropriate risk profile of the customer [5].
reference_answer: When info in CKYCR has changed, record is incomplete or not per current norms, validity of documents has lapsed, or RE needs to verify identity/address or perform EDD.

question: Can a customer whose OVD shows a Delhi address open an account in Chennai, and what must they do within 3 months?
answer:
A customer whose OVD shows a Delhi address can open an account in Chennai [4]. However, in case of a change only in the address details of the customer, a self-declaration of the new address shall be obtained from the customer [4]. The declared address shall be verified through positive confirmation within two months [4]. 

The provided context does not contain enough information to answer what the customer must do within 3 months fully.
reference_answer: Yes, by submitting a deemed OVD for proof of address. Within 3 months they must submit an OVD with current address.

question: Is Aadhaar mandatory for KYC, and can it be used for non-face-to-face periodic KYC updation?
answer: Aadhaar is not mandatory for KYC [4]. However, Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC can be used for non-face-to-face mode account opening [1] and for periodic KYC updation [2]. In case of updation/ periodic updation of KYC through Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face to face mode, the conditions stipulated in paragraph 17 are not applicable [2]. REs shall ensure that the mobile number for Aadhaar authentication is the same as the one available with them in the customer’s profile [2].
reference_answer: Aadhaar is not mandatory unless receiving government subsidies under Section 7. For NF2F periodic updation, Aadhaar OTP-based e-KYC is one of the permitted modes.

question: What is the beneficial ownership threshold for a company versus a trust?
answer: For a company, the beneficial ownership threshold is more than 10 percent of the shares or capital or profits of the company [4]. In contrast, for a trust, the identification of beneficial owner(s) shall include identification of the author of the trust, the trustee, the beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust [1]. Additionally, for a partnership firm, the beneficial owner is the natural person(s) who has/have ownership of/entitlement to more than 10 percent of capital or profits of the partnership [1], whereas for an unincorporated association or body of individuals, the beneficial owner is the natural person(s) who has/have ownership of/entitlement to more than 15 percent of the property or capital or profits [1].
reference_answer: Company: more than 10% shareholding/capital/profits. Trust: author, trustee, beneficiaries with 10% or more interest, and any person with ultimate effective control.

question: What warning has RBI issued about KYC fraud and what law allows account closure for non-compliance?
answer: The RBI has issued a warning to prevent frauds in non-face-to-face customer onboarding, including not linking alternate mobile numbers post CDD [4]. For accounts opened using Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode, if the aggregate balance exceeds rupees one lakh, the account shall cease to be operational till CDD is complete [1]. Furthermore, if the CDD procedure is not completed within a year, in respect of deposit accounts, the same shall be closed immediately [1]. The RBI has also mandated that REs shall have strict monitoring procedures to ensure compliance with the conditions for such accounts [1]. The law allowing account closure for non-compliance is not explicitly stated in the provided context, but the RBI's Master Direction KYC 2016, updated through 2025, outlines the guidelines for account closure due to non-compliance [1].
reference_answer: RBI warned against clicking suspicious links in KYC SMS/emails (Press Release Sep 13, 2021). PMLA Rules 2005 enable account closure after due notice for non-compliance.

question: Can a KYC or periodic KYC updation application be automatically rejected, and what is the rule for joint accounts?
answer: No application for onboarding or periodic updation of KYC shall be rejected without application of mind [1]. For joint accounts, the CDD Procedure is to be followed for all the joint account holders, while opening a joint account [4].
reference_answer: Automated rejection is not permitted; decisions must be reviewed by an authorised RE official. All joint account holders must submit KYC documents individually.

question: Can an NRI submit KYC documents without visiting a branch, and who can certify their OVD copies?
answer: An NRI can submit KYC documents without visiting a branch through non-face-to-face modes [1]. For non-face-to-face customer onboarding, obtaining a copy of OVD certified by additional certifying authorities as allowed for NRIs and PIOs is permitted [1]. However, the specific details of who can certify the OVD copies for NRIs are not provided in the given context.
reference_answer: Yes, NRIs can use non-face-to-face onboarding. OVD copies can be certified by overseas branches of Indian SCBs, notary public, court magistrate, judge, or Indian embassy/consulate.
